---
title: "Chapter 22 — 🔗 Graph Neural Networks"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 21 · 🌫️ Diffusion Models](ch21.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [23 · 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations →](ch23.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Most real-world data does not arrive as a neat grid of pixels or an orderly sequence of words. It arrives as a **graph**: a set of entities (nodes) joined by relationships (edges). Social networks, molecules, road maps, citation networks, and recommendation systems are all graphs at heart. Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are the family of deep learning models built to learn directly on this structure, by letting each node gather information from its neighbors. They sit in deep learning alongside CNNs and Transformers, but generalize the idea of "local" computation from fixed grids to arbitrary connectivity.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Deep learning for graph-structured data · used for node classification, link prediction, and molecule/graph-level prediction · the one key idea is **message passing**: each node repeatedly updates its representation by aggregating information from its neighbors.
:::

## 22.1 — Why graphs need their own models

A picture is a grid: every pixel has exactly the same number of neighbors, arranged in the same positions (up, down, left, right). A sentence is a sequence: every token has a clear "before" and "after." This regularity is what convolutions and recurrence exploit — a CNN slides the *same* small filter across every location precisely because every location looks structurally identical.

Graphs break that assumption. The geometric contrast is the whole story: on a grid every node has the same fixed, ordered neighborhood; on a graph the neighborhood varies in size and has no canonical order.

<svg viewBox="0 0 460 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11">
  <text x="60" y="18" font-weight="bold">grid: identical neighbors</text>
  <g stroke="#888" stroke-width="1">
    <line x1="40" y1="60" x2="160" y2="60"/><line x1="40" y1="100" x2="160" y2="100"/><line x1="40" y1="140" x2="160" y2="140"/>
    <line x1="40" y1="60" x2="40" y2="140"/><line x1="100" y1="60" x2="100" y2="140"/><line x1="160" y1="60" x2="160" y2="140"/>
  </g>
  <g fill="#5b8def">
    <circle cx="40" cy="60" r="6"/><circle cx="100" cy="60" r="6"/><circle cx="160" cy="60" r="6"/>
    <circle cx="40" cy="100" r="6"/><circle cx="160" cy="100" r="6"/>
    <circle cx="40" cy="140" r="6"/><circle cx="100" cy="140" r="6"/><circle cx="160" cy="140" r="6"/>
  </g>
  <circle cx="100" cy="100" r="8" fill="#e8694a"/>
  <text x="300" y="18" font-weight="bold" text-anchor="middle">graph: variable, unordered</text>
  <g stroke="#888" stroke-width="1">
    <line x1="300" y1="100" x2="240" y2="55"/><line x1="300" y1="100" x2="370" y2="50"/>
    <line x1="300" y1="100" x2="395" y2="110"/><line x1="300" y1="100" x2="350" y2="160"/>
    <line x1="300" y1="100" x2="250" y2="150"/><line x1="370" y1="50" x2="395" y2="110"/>
  </g>
  <g fill="#5b8def">
    <circle cx="240" cy="55" r="6"/><circle cx="370" cy="50" r="6"/><circle cx="395" cy="110" r="6"/>
    <circle cx="350" cy="160" r="6"/><circle cx="250" cy="150" r="6"/>
  </g>
  <circle cx="300" cy="100" r="8" fill="#e8694a"/>
</svg>

A **graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a set of nodes $V$ and edges $E \subseteq V \times V$. Two things make it awkward for grid/sequence models:

- **Variable neighborhood size.** One node may have 2 neighbors, another 2,000. There is no fixed-size patch to convolve.
- **No canonical ordering.** The neighbors of a node form a *set*, not a list. If you relabel the nodes, it is still the same graph. A good graph model must be **permutation-invariant** (or equivariant): its output must not depend on the arbitrary order in which you happened to number the nodes.

The standard algebraic description of a graph is the **adjacency matrix** $A \in \{0,1\}^{n \times n}$, where $A_{ij}=1$ if there is an edge from $i$ to $j$. Edges can be **directed** (the relation runs one way, as in "A cites B") or **undirected** (the relation is mutual, as in "A is friends with B"), and an undirected graph has a *symmetric* adjacency matrix, $A = A^\top$. The basic GNN machinery below — and almost every example in this chapter, from social networks to molecules to citation graphs — assumes the undirected, symmetric case; the symmetric normalization in GCN (Section 22.3) relies on it directly. Each node also carries a feature vector; stacked together they form the node feature matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$.

You might be tempted to just flatten $A$ and $X$ and feed them to a multilayer perceptron. That fails badly: it is not permutation-invariant (shuffling node ids changes the input but not the graph), and it cannot generalize to graphs of a different size. GNNs solve both problems by computing in terms of the *local neighborhood structure* rather than absolute positions.

::: {.callout-tip}
The unifying test for a sound graph operation: if you renumber the nodes, the predictions should follow the renumbering and otherwise stay identical. Any layer built from "aggregate over the neighbor set" automatically passes this test, because sets have no order.
:::

## 22.2 — Message passing: the core mechanism

The single idea behind almost every modern GNN is **message passing** (also called neighborhood aggregation). The intuition is gossip on a social network: in each round, every person updates what they know by listening to their immediate friends; after several rounds, information has rippled out from many hops away. A node's final representation summarizes its whole local neighborhood.

Formally, each node $v$ holds a hidden vector $h_v$. One message-passing layer updates every node in parallel through three steps:

1. **Message** — each neighbor $u$ of $v$ produces a message, often just its current state $h_u$ (possibly transformed by the edge between them, via an edge feature $e_{uv}$ such as a bond type or a relation label).
2. **Aggregate** — the messages from the neighbor set $\mathcal{N}(v)$ are combined with a *permutation-invariant* reduction (sum, mean, or max).
3. **Update** — the node combines the aggregated message with its own previous state to form the new state.

In symbols, for layer $k$:

$$
h_v^{(k)} = \text{UPDATE}\!\left( h_v^{(k-1)},\; \bigoplus_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)} \text{MESSAGE}\big(h_u^{(k-1)}, h_v^{(k-1)}, e_{uv}\big) \right)
$$

where $\bigoplus$ is the permutation-invariant aggregator. Stacking $K$ such layers gives each node a **receptive field** of $K$ hops: after $K$ rounds, $h_v$ depends on every node reachable within $K$ steps. This is the direct analogue of stacking convolutions to widen a CNN's receptive field.

Here is one message-passing round on a tiny graph, computing the new state of the center node A from its neighbors B, C, D:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  B["h_B"] -->|message| AGG(("+ aggregate"))
  C["h_C"] -->|message| AGG
  D["h_D"] -->|message| AGG
  A0["h_A old"] --> UPD["update"]
  AGG --> UPD
  UPD --> A1["h_A new"]
```

A minimal from-scratch version makes the mechanics concrete — one mean-aggregation layer over an adjacency matrix:

```python
import numpy as np

def gnn_layer(H, A, W):
    # H: (n,d) node states, A: (n,n) adjacency, W: (d,d') weights
    A_hat = A + np.eye(A.shape[0])      # add self-loops: keep your own info
    deg = A_hat.sum(axis=1, keepdims=True)
    msg = A_hat @ H                      # sum neighbor (and self) states
    agg = msg / deg                      # ponytail: row-mean (GraphSAGE-mean style),
                                         # NOT the symmetric GCN norm of 22.3
    return np.maximum(agg @ W, 0)        # update: linear + ReLU
```

::: {.callout-warning}
The aggregator **must** be order-independent. Reaching for an RNN or a plain concatenation to combine neighbors silently smuggles in an ordering, breaking permutation invariance and making the model depend on arbitrary node ids. Stick to sum, mean, or max.
:::

## 22.3 — Graph Convolutional Networks (GCN)

The **Graph Convolutional Network** (GCN) is the simplest and most influential instance of message passing. Its layer is a single tidy formula:

$$
H^{(k)} = \sigma\!\left( \hat{D}^{-1/2}\, \hat{A}\, \hat{D}^{-1/2}\, H^{(k-1)} W^{(k)} \right)
$$

Unpacking it from the intuition outward: $\hat{A} = A + I$ adds a **self-loop** so each node keeps its own features alongside its neighbors'. $\hat{D}$ is the diagonal degree matrix of $\hat{A}$. The term $\hat{D}^{-1/2}\hat{A}\hat{D}^{-1/2}$ is **symmetric normalization**: it weights the edge between nodes $i$ and $j$ by $1/\sqrt{d_i d_j}$, where $d_i, d_j$ are their degrees. This is a *degree-normalized weighted sum*, not a plain mean — each row does not sum to one, and high-degree hubs are damped on both ends of every edge so a single richly connected node does not dominate every neighbor it touches. $W^{(k)}$ is a learned linear projection shared across all nodes (the analogue of a CNN filter being shared across positions), and $\sigma$ is a nonlinearity like ReLU.

A useful way to see GCN: it is the same message-passing template as 22.2, but with this specific symmetric, degree-aware edge weighting instead of the simpler row-mean used in that section's code. It is applied to every node simultaneously via one sparse matrix multiply, and that sparsity is what makes it efficient — cost scales with the number of *edges*, not $n^2$.

GCN's strength is simplicity and strong baselines on **transductive** problems, where the whole graph (including the nodes you will be tested on) is available at training time, as in citation networks. Its weaknesses: the edge weights are fixed by degree rather than learned, every neighbor in a node's neighborhood is treated as equally relevant (up to its degree weight), and the full-graph matrix multiply assumes the entire graph fits in memory — which fails for graphs with billions of edges.

## 22.4 — GraphSAGE: sampling for scale

GCN's "multiply by the whole adjacency matrix" recipe assumes you can hold the entire graph in memory and that every node you will ever score was present during training. Neither holds for a billion-edge social graph where new users appear every second. **GraphSAGE** (SAmple and aggreGatE) fixes both by changing two things: it *samples* a fixed number of neighbors instead of using all of them, and it *learns an aggregator function* rather than a fixed normalization.

The intuition: to compute an embedding for a node, you do not need to listen to all 5,000 of its neighbors — a random sample of, say, 25 gives a good enough estimate of the neighborhood, at bounded cost. For a $K$-layer model you sample a fixed fan-out at each hop, so the computation per node is bounded regardless of how dense the graph is.

The per-layer update separates neighbor aggregation from the self-update:

$$
h_v^{(k)} = \sigma\!\left( W^{(k)} \cdot \text{CONCAT}\!\Big( h_v^{(k-1)},\; \text{AGG}_k\big(\{ h_u^{(k-1)} : u \in \mathcal{S}(v)\}\big) \Big) \right)
$$

where $\mathcal{S}(v)$ is a sampled subset of $v$'s neighbors and $\text{AGG}_k$ can be mean, max-pooling, or an LSTM applied to a randomly-permuted neighbor list. Concatenating the node's own previous state keeps self-information distinct from neighbor information rather than blending them.

The decisive property is that GraphSAGE is **inductive**: because it learns *functions* (aggregate, then transform) rather than a fixed embedding per node, it can produce embeddings for nodes — or entire graphs — never seen during training. That is what makes it deployable on graphs that grow over time.

| | GCN | GraphSAGE | GAT |
|---|---|---|---|
| Neighbor weighting | fixed (degree normalization) | uniform over sampled set | learned attention |
| Uses all neighbors? | yes (full graph) | no (samples a fixed fan-out) | yes |
| Inductive? | weakly | yes | yes |
| Main cost driver | number of edges | sample size × layers | edges × attention heads |
| Best when | mid-size transductive graphs | huge / growing graphs | neighbor importance varies |

## 22.5 — Graph Attention and Graph Isomorphism Networks

GCN decides how much a neighbor matters purely from node degree; GraphSAGE treats sampled neighbors uniformly. But often some neighbors genuinely matter more than others — a paper's most relevant citation, a molecule's bonding partner. **Graph Attention Networks** (GAT) let the model *learn* those weights, by importing the attention mechanism (Chapter 18) into the neighborhood aggregation step.

The idea: for each edge, compute an **attention coefficient** $\alpha_{vu}$ saying how much node $v$ should attend to neighbor $u$, then aggregate neighbors as an attention-weighted sum instead of a plain mean. The coefficients are computed from the node features themselves and normalized across each node's neighborhood with a softmax:

$$
\alpha_{vu} = \frac{\exp\!\big(\text{LeakyReLU}(a^\top [W h_v \,\Vert\, W h_u])\big)}{\sum_{w \in \mathcal{N}(v)} \exp\!\big(\text{LeakyReLU}(a^\top [W h_v \,\Vert\, W h_w])\big)}, \qquad h_v' = \sigma\!\Big(\sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)} \alpha_{vu}\, W h_u\Big)
$$

Here $\Vert$ is concatenation and $a$ is a small learned attention vector. As with Transformers, GAT typically uses **multiple attention heads** in parallel and concatenates (or averages) their outputs, so the model can attend to different kinds of relationships at once.

GAT shines when neighbor importance is heterogeneous and you want the weighting to be data-driven rather than structural. The coefficients also offer a modest interpretability bonus: you can inspect which neighbors a node leaned on. The costs are extra parameters and compute per edge, and — like all attention — sensitivity to how the scores are normalized.

A different design goal motivates the **Graph Isomorphism Network** (GIN). Rather than learning *which* neighbors matter, GIN asks how to make message passing as *discriminative* as possible — able to tell apart graphs that are genuinely structurally different. Its answer is to aggregate with a plain **sum** (not mean or max) and pass the result through an MLP:

$$
h_v^{(k)} = \text{MLP}^{(k)}\!\Big( (1 + \epsilon)\, h_v^{(k-1)} + \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}(v)} h_u^{(k-1)} \Big)
$$

Sum aggregation is the key: mean and max throw away multiplicity (two neighbors that look the same as one), whereas sum preserves counts. This makes GIN provably as powerful as the Weisfeiler–Lehman test discussed in 22.7 — the theoretical ceiling for this style of model. It is the standard strong baseline for graph-level tasks where fine structural distinctions matter.

## 22.6 — Node, edge, and graph-level tasks

A trained GNN produces an embedding for every node. What you do with those embeddings depends on the **level** of the prediction task, and it is worth being explicit about the three levels because they change the model's head, the loss, and how you split data.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  G["Input graph + node features"] --> MP["Stacked message-passing layers"]
  MP --> NE["Node embeddings"]
  NE -->|per node| NT["Node-level: classify / regress each node"]
  NE -->|per pair| ET["Edge-level: does an edge exist? score it"]
  NE -->|pool all| GT["Graph-level: one label for the whole graph"]
```

**Node-level** tasks attach a classifier or regressor to each node embedding — for example, labeling each account in a network as fraud or legitimate. **Edge-level** (link prediction) tasks score *pairs* of node embeddings to predict whether an edge should exist; this is the engine behind "people you may know" and many recommender systems, typically scoring a pair with a dot product or a small network over $[h_u \Vert h_v]$. **Graph-level** tasks produce one prediction for an entire graph — is this molecule toxic? — and require collapsing all node embeddings into a single graph vector, which is the job of a readout (next section).

A subtlety unique to graphs is the train/test split. In **transductive** settings the graph is fixed and you mask out some nodes' labels for testing while still using their *features and edges* during message passing. In **inductive** settings you test on entirely separate graphs. Confusing the two is a common source of inflated results.

## 22.7 — Readout, pooling, and the over-smoothing trap

For graph-level prediction you need a **readout** (or **graph pooling**) function that turns a variable number of node embeddings into one fixed-size vector. Like neighbor aggregation, it must be permutation-invariant, so the standard choices are again sum, mean, or max over all nodes:

$$
h_G = \bigoplus_{v \in V} h_v^{(K)}
$$

Sum-pooling preserves information about graph *size* and total counts (useful for molecules); mean-pooling is size-normalized; max-pooling captures the single most salient node. More elaborate schemes learn a *hierarchical* pooling that coarsens the graph in stages, mimicking how a CNN downsamples an image — but a plain global sum or mean is a strong, cheap default.

The most important failure mode to understand is **over-smoothing**. Intuition: message passing is the $\bigoplus$ aggregator from 22.2 applied over and over, and when that aggregator is an average, repeated averaging is exactly how things blur together — the same reason a photo smeared many times turns uniformly gray. Algebraically, repeatedly multiplying node features by the normalized adjacency matrix drives them toward its dominant eigenvector, so the component that distinguishes one node from another shrinks geometrically with depth. Stack too many layers and every node's embedding converges toward the same value — the whole graph turns into mush and nodes become indistinguishable. The SVG below sketches this: distinct node colors at layer 0 wash into a single shade by layer 3.

<svg viewBox="0 0 420 150" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11">
  <text x="40" y="20" text-anchor="middle">layer 0</text>
  <text x="170" y="20" text-anchor="middle">layer 1</text>
  <text x="300" y="20" text-anchor="middle">layer 2</text>
  <text x="395" y="20" text-anchor="middle">layer 3</text>
  <g>
    <circle cx="40" cy="50" r="9" fill="#e8694a"/><circle cx="40" cy="80" r="9" fill="#5b8def"/><circle cx="40" cy="110" r="9" fill="#3cb371"/>
    <circle cx="170" cy="50" r="9" fill="#d77a5e"/><circle cx="170" cy="80" r="9" fill="#6f8fc9"/><circle cx="170" cy="110" r="9" fill="#5fa57f"/>
    <circle cx="300" cy="50" r="9" fill="#b6897c"/><circle cx="300" cy="80" r="9" fill="#8b95b0"/><circle cx="300" cy="110" r="9" fill="#86977f"/>
    <circle cx="395" cy="50" r="9" fill="#9a9088"/><circle cx="395" cy="80" r="9" fill="#979088"/><circle cx="395" cy="110" r="9" fill="#969088"/>
  </g>
</svg>

This is why GNNs are usually *shallow* (2–4 layers), in sharp contrast to the dozens or hundreds of layers in CNNs. The tension is real: you need depth to grow the receptive field and reach distant nodes, but depth drives over-smoothing. The usual mitigations:

- **Residual / skip connections** so a node retains its own earlier features.
- **Jumping-knowledge** connections that combine embeddings from *all* layers rather than only the last.
- **Normalization** (PairNorm and similar) that explicitly keeps node embeddings from collapsing together.
- Simply **keeping the network shallow** and widening the sampled neighborhood instead.

::: {.callout-warning}
"More layers = more power" is a CNN instinct that backfires on graphs. Past a handful of message-passing layers, accuracy usually *drops* from over-smoothing. If a deep GNN underperforms, suspect over-smoothing before you suspect underfitting.
:::

A related limit worth naming is **expressive power**: standard message-passing GNNs are at most as discriminative as the **Weisfeiler–Lehman** (WL) graph isomorphism test, meaning some structurally different graphs produce identical embeddings. GIN (Section 22.5) is the architecture that reaches this ceiling exactly, via sum aggregation plus an MLP. It is a theoretical bound, but a practical reminder that not every graph distinction is learnable by neighborhood aggregation alone.

## 22.8 — Where GNNs are used

GNNs earn their keep wherever the relationships between entities carry as much signal as the entities themselves:

- **Chemistry and drug discovery.** Molecules are graphs (atoms = nodes, bonds = edges). Graph-level models predict solubility, toxicity, and binding affinity; this is one of the most successful real-world GNN domains. Bond types are *edge features*, which vanilla GCN/GAT ignore unless you extend them to use $e_{uv}$.
- **Recommendation.** User–item interactions form a bipartite graph; link prediction over it powers large-scale recommenders (web-scale GraphSAGE variants are deployed in production).
- **Social and fraud networks.** Node classification flags fraudulent accounts or bots by how they connect, not just their individual features — relational signal that tabular models miss.
- **Knowledge graphs.** Here edges have *types* (relations like "born-in" or "works-for"), and plain GCN/GAT — which treat every edge alike — cannot represent that natively. Relational variants such as **R-GCN** give each relation type its own transformation; these are the right tool for typed-edge link prediction and question answering (see Chapter 39).
- **Physics, traffic, and combinatorics.** Particle interactions, road networks, and routing problems are naturally relational and benefit from message passing.

The common thread: when an edge encodes real information — a bond, a friendship, a citation, a purchase — encoding that structure directly tends to beat flattening the graph into a feature table and throwing away the connections.

## 22.x — Key takeaways

- Graphs have variable-size, unordered neighborhoods, so grid/sequence models do not fit; GNN layers must be **permutation-invariant**. Most GNN basics assume an undirected graph (symmetric $A$).
- **Message passing** — message, then permutation-invariant **aggregate**, then **update** — is the engine behind nearly all GNNs; stacking $K$ layers gives a $K$-hop receptive field.
- **GCN** is a degree-normalized weighted sum ($1/\sqrt{d_i d_j}$ per edge — not a plain mean); **GraphSAGE** samples neighbors and learns aggregators to scale and go **inductive**; **GAT** learns per-neighbor **attention** weights; **GIN** uses sum + MLP to reach the WL expressiveness ceiling.
- Tasks come at three levels — **node**, **edge** (link prediction), and **graph** — and graph-level tasks need a permutation-invariant **readout** (sum/mean/max pooling).
- GNNs are usually **shallow** because stacking layers causes **over-smoothing**, where repeated averaging drags all node embeddings toward one value; residuals, jumping-knowledge, and normalization help.
- Vanilla GCN/GAT ignore **edge and relation types**; typed-edge graphs like knowledge graphs need extensions such as R-GCN.
- Use GNNs when the edges carry real signal: molecules, recommendation, fraud, knowledge graphs, and physical/relational systems.

## 22.y — See also

- **Chapter 18 — Attention & Transformers** — the attention mechanism that GAT adapts to neighborhoods; Transformers can be viewed as GNNs on a fully connected graph.
- **Chapter 16 — Convolutional Neural Networks** — the grid-based convolution and receptive-field ideas that GNNs generalize to arbitrary graphs.
- **Chapter 35 — Recommender Systems** — link prediction and bipartite user–item graphs, a major GNN application.
- **Chapter 39 — Knowledge Representation & Reasoning** — knowledge graphs and relational reasoning that graph models operate over.
- **Chapter 10 — Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning** — node embeddings as learned low-dimensional representations of graph structure.
- **Chapter 15 — Training Deep Neural Networks** — the skip connections, normalization, and optimization tricks that GNNs borrow to fight over-smoothing.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 21 · 🌫️ Diffusion Models](ch21.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [23 · 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations →](ch23.html)
